Alright, so let's get into your Facebook ad copy. So, and learn how to master the text of your Facebook ad. So you've got a nice picture, right? But you just need to know what to say now in your copy. And Facebook, depending on the ad, I mean you've got a headline and a sub headline and sometimes even a third one where you can describe the link a little bit more. So we need to have some good, some good text on what to say. Okay, so, I mean the number one thing with copy, until you're an expert copy writer, go out there and swipe it, right? Find, find copy out there and make it your own. Good examples, I'll show you some good examples here on yahoo.com. Where to find some easy swipe copy there on Google Suggest, on Google Ads, and Facebook itself. So let's come over here. First, let's go yahoo. The reason I bring this up is yahoo's news feed here is always full of really captivating stories. I mean, some of them might not be relevant, so you just keep moving through, but let's see what we can find. I mean, I'm totally doing this ad lib here. So, little girl who bonded with the NCAA star dies. I mean, it's emotional. Tales of heroism emerging from a school stabbing. I mean, that's really unfortunate. But here's one, how much to tip in every situation? Okay, so maybe you don't know that. That's really curiosity based, how much to tip in every situation. I'm curious, I'm going to click, right? You can see the subhead here and make note of what they're saying here as our subhead line that as well as their call to action link. These yahoo article writers, whoever they are, do a great job. Words, five phrases you should never put on your resume. That's kind of cool. It's short, I know that there's only five things. I can click through five things really fast and see them so I know that this is a fast article that I can consume quickly. There's some kind of a threat involved here. What's going to happen to me if I do put those things on my resume? Oh my goodness, I better check it out. There's these current events things which are great. This one's interesting, 101 year old message in a bottle finally arrives. This one's great because it's curiosity based, it's pulling up things that everyone's heard about, messages in a bottle and this one's got history behind it. The picture's interesting, I noticed it right as I scrolled by, what is that weird bottle? Things like that. Anyway, just scroll through here and get some great ideas for some swipe copy, the ones you like, save them. Copy them and save them and then in the future you can use pieces of them to understand how to phrase your headlines. Let's look at Google Suggest now. So if we go to Google, Google Suggest is what comes up when you type in this bar. So let's do a golf product. So we're going to improve our golf swing. So if I just start typing in golf, I can immediately start to see what other people tend to type in when they type in golf. Google's showing me the popular ones. I can get more specific, get even more ideas. So golf swing videos are popular, golf swing tips are popular, golf swing analyzers and mechanics and shirts are popular. So I can use this in my ad copy by deciding whether or not I want to promote the fact that I have a video on my page or that I have tips on my page or that I can teach you the golf swing mechanics or things like that. So let me just do different versions of seed keywords and figure out what people are looking for. That way you can write a headline that speaks directly to them. So improving your golf swing is big. Increasing my golf swing, improving your golf swing driver, improve your driver, golf swing with drills. So yeah, drills, that's interesting. So that's really specific. So how to, six drills on how to improve your golf swing, six drills you can do today to improve your golf swing by tomorrow, you know, whatever. So using Google's suggest is a cool way to do that. So once you have a good buyer keyword like this, search and here in Google itself you can see all the ads, right? All these ads that are running and all these ads that are running. Well there's some more copy for you. So here's somebody, you know, they're running these ads and if these ads stick around for more than, you know, more than just a really short term, you know that they're working. That's why they're there. So even these top organic searches, I mean they're there because people are interacting with them and searching with them. So effective headlines here. So top three golf training aids, surprising new training, aid that shocks the status quo, right? So that's really good. So you can easily add an additional 12 to 16 miles per hour to your drive and get up to 40 yards off the tee. It's a really good copy here. So you can swipe all this and add it to your, you know, your copy swipe list. So you've got ideas on when you're writing your headlines. So anyway, even on Facebook itself, so what you can do is you can go and like pages. And join groups and stuff that are on the topic that you want and make sure you know, you're following and liking and joining all the things that are similar to your niche. That way you're going to see in your newsfeed, you're going to see ads of your competitors. And that way you've got a lot of swipe copy there too. So tons of places to find it. There are three categories of social headlines, right? And I'll credit this over to recent posts on digital marketer, but three categories of social headlines. I really liked the way they broke it down. Social proof or piggybacking threat, which is pain or fear of loss and gain or benefit and promise category. So the social proof one, for example, think things like reviews, Amazon reviews. Have you ever gone to Amazon? And before you bought that gadget, you checked out the reviews and saw that most people were four or five stars. You read a few of them and then you realized, yeah, it really is good. So you bought it, right? So that's social proof you're playing with there with reviews. Testimonials are more examples of social proof referrals. I mean, social proof in the real world is if somebody comes up to you, you know, or you mentioned that you really need a dentist and your buddy says to you, I know the best dentist in town. That's a direct social proof referral word of mouth type thing. So that's what social proof is. Kind of a note to remember with social proof is the more people that are making that choice and the more influential that person or people are, it's the stronger the social proof. So if I tell you, as your buddy, if I tell you that this is the best toothpaste in town, you know, in the market, then it delivers one level of social proof. But if your dentist, who I would say in that regard, in that context is very influential, if your dentist says this is the best toothpaste in the market you should buy, then think of how much more weight that carries, right? So how many people and how influential are the people who are given the social proof? So social proof headline, some examples. So with this in mind, piggyback being writing on, you know, on a celebrity or an influential person and social proof being just people, lots of people, reviews, testimonials, people you trust, stuff like that. So an example of social proof. Why thousands of New Yorkers threw their cell phones and their gutter on Friday? Why the heck did they do that? Obviously a lot of people did it. I got to figure out why I'm going to click, right? An example of piggybacking social proof is what Dr. Oz eats for a midnight snack, right? Dr. Oz, the guru of health. And what's he eating for a midnight snack? Maybe I should copy him. So here we are piggybacking on his reputation for social proof. So that's one category of social headlines, a Facebook ad headlines that you could use. So use kind of a social proof angle. Here's another one. Here's another category, threats or pain or fear of loss. To basically the idea that if you take action, you can avoid some impending doom or some imminent pain or keep what you would otherwise lose, okay? So here's some examples of threat, pain or fear of loss ads. So an example, what the banks aren't telling you and how you can save hundreds every month. So you're going to lose hundreds every month. There's pain in your ignorance. You got to click, right? So the next one, warning, right there is a threat. Warning, don't eat another vegetable without reading this. Don't take another pill without reading this. Don't do this without clicking here, right? So it's a warning threat. The next one, do you really know what lurks inside your protein powder? Dangerous ingredients revealed. So here's a threat that you don't know what's in there until you read this thing. So you've got to click. So those are really, really interesting. What's interesting thing with human nature and threats and pain is that oftentimes we tend to take action more to avoid loss and we do take action to receive gain. So keep that in mind when you try different headlines. Definitely try a threat headline or a fear of loss headline as well as a gain headline. Here's some ideas behind the gain headline. So basically the idea is simply give a benefit and to call the action, implying that if they take that action, they will gain the benefit, okay? Some examples would be click here for six tips for creating content that actually gets read, right? So take this action, you'll gain the benefit. The next one, how to lose 15 pounds in 15 days, a secret revealed. So implied that if you click here, you're going to lose or at least know how to lose 15 pounds in 15 days. So a simple benefit or direct obvious benefit with the call to action implying or actually stating that if you take that action, you'll gain that benefit. So that's what a gain headline is. A great for Facebook ads as well. So those are your three categories. Now let me go into some extra tips and tricks here for you. So trick one is to speed up your copy. Okay, we all know that people are busy and distracted and they don't want to commit very much by clicking on your ad. So help them understand that it will be really quick to consume. That way you're going to get more clicks to your landing page. So if your ad were to say something like learn how to cook artichokes the right way, well gosh, I don't know how long that's how much time that's I'm going to have to dedicate to learn how to cook artichokes the right way. Maybe it's a 10 page article. Maybe I have to read a whole book on it. I don't even know, right? But if I were to speed up this headline, I could do so by saying seven quick tips to cooking artichokes like the pros. Right, so I know that there's seven bullets. If I clicked through here, sure there's probably paragraphs with each bullet, but I could probably just skim through and see the seven tips and be on my way. So I've sped up the copy even though I'm busy and distracted. There's enough of a benefit here that I want to grab that I'll do it as long as there's only seven. Let's tip trick number one is to speed up your copy. Trick number two is using a quote from Ralph Waldo Emerson, curiosity is lying in wait for every secret. So a curiosity based copy, I learned this from Russell Brunson. He taught me that curiosity based headlines convert. So take a gain or a threat, right, or even social proof I guess, but a gain or a threat and attach it to something bizarre or unrelated. It teases readers with your curiosity. So for example, I mean I could have an ad that says lose weight and gain muscle mass seemingly overnight. Sure, it seems like a good headline. Or I can add curiosity to it, which might really get a lot more clicks and it says, how a prisoner of war in order to survive learned the secrets of rapid muscle gain seemingly overnight, right? So now you've got a story. It's interesting. I'm curious how the heck can a prisoner of war do that. So I'm going to click and then a curiosity is going to drive those clicks. And so this is just a good overview of Facebook ad copy. Again, go back to your sources and swipe and see what people are doing. Swipe all the copy you can. Modify it. Make it your own. Use the right phrases. You know, go to Yahoo and look at their ads or their news feed that's always cycling. I mean, there's always a hundred, you know, fifty to a hundred new articles up there every day that you can cycle through to get good swipe copy ideas. Also like I said, you're doing this for Facebook. So you might as well get into Facebook. Make sure you have tagged yourself as somebody who's interested in the topic that you're building ads for so that you can see all the ads of your competitors, right? If you're in weight loss, we'll make sure that you like Dr. Oz and Oprah and all these weight loss books and you watch The Biggest Loser and, you know, make yourself the person who's the target audience for your competitors so that their ads will show up in your news feed and on your right hand side. That way you can swipe copy from Facebook too. And again, always use Google. Google has ads running all the time. Just type in your keyword and you get a whole bunch of ads to look at and swipe copy from. So anyway, I hope this helps and that you have some great understanding on how to write the simple copy that the Facebook ads take.
